name: MCP Server Bash SDK
what_it_does: MCP Server Bash SDK enabling developers to build MCP servers using pure Bash shell scripts — providing a lightweight SDK for wrapping existing shell scripts, CLI tools, and Unix commands as MCP tools, making it easy to expose shell-based functionality to AI agents without requiring Python or Node.js.
use_cases: Wrapping existing shell scripts as MCP tools from DevOps teams
Exposing CLI tools to AI agents using familiar Bash scripting
Building MCP servers on minimal systems without Python/Node.js runtime
Creating lightweight MCP tools for Unix system administration from SRE teams
Rapidly prototyping MCP tools using existing shell commands
Building MCP servers for embedded or resource-constrained environments
not_for: Complex data processing (Bash has limitations for complex logic)
Teams needing typed interfaces or structured error handling
best_when: A DevOps or SRE team has existing shell scripts and CLI tools they want to expose as MCP tools without writing Python or Node.js code.
avoid_when: You need complex data processing, typed interfaces, or rich error handling — use Python or Go MCP SDKs instead.

auth.notes: No SDK-level auth — shell scripts handle auth as needed. MCP server runs with shell user's permissions.

pricing.notes: Free, open source. Bash is pre-installed on Unix/macOS.

performance.rate_limits: Local — no rate limits; shell command execution overhead

known_agent_gotchas: Shell injection risk — never pass unsanitized agent input to shell commands
Error handling in Bash is limited — exit codes may not provide rich error context
Shell script execution has higher process overhead than in-process SDKs
Bash portability issues — scripts may behave differently on macOS vs Linux
Complex input/output schemas difficult to implement cleanly in Bash
Secrets in environment variables are visible to child processes
agent_readiness.security_notes: CRITICAL: Shell injection risk — sanitize all agent inputs before passing to shell. Scripts run with user permissions. Secrets in env vars are visible to child processes. Use with caution in multi-user environments. Community SDK.

security_score_components.security_notes: Shell injection risk is critical. Sanitize all inputs. Env var secrets visible to subprocesses. User-level permissions. Community SDK.
